title.en: Gemini for Workspace & the Google Enterprise Stack

description.en: See how Gemini embeds into Gmail, Docs, Sheets and Meet, how Workspace licensing tiers gate features, and how the Workspace + Vertex combination forms Google's full enterprise AI stack.

story_variants.en:
## AI Where the Work Already Lives

Anthropic and OpenAI sell destinations — you open Claude or ChatGPT and bring your work to the AI. Google's workforce strategy inverts this: **Gemini comes to where the work already is.** The side panel in Gmail drafts replies with your thread as context; Docs offers "Help me write" inside the document; Sheets generates formulas and analyzes tables; Meet takes notes and summarizes meetings; Drive-wide Q&A answers questions across your files; and NotebookLM turns document collections into research assistants (audio overviews included). Distribution is the strategy: for organizations already on Workspace, there is nothing new to open, and adoption friction approaches zero.

That is a genuine advantage — and it comes with genuine trade-offs this lesson will name honestly.

## The Context Advantage (and Its Boundary)

Embedded AI wins on *ambient context*: Gemini in Gmail can ground a draft in the actual thread; Drive Q&A can cite the actual documents. The critical governance property making this safe is **permission inheritance** — Gemini answers only from content the *asking user* can already access. Google's enterprise commitments here mirror the industry standard: Workspace business data is **not used to train models outside your organization**, and interactions stay within the Workspace trust boundary covered by existing compliance certifications.

> **Callout:** Permission inheritance is only as good as your permissions. Companies with years of "anyone-with-link" sharing sludge discover that AI-powered search *surfaces* over-shared files ruthlessly. A Drive permissions audit belongs *before* the Gemini rollout, not after the first awkward discovery.

## Licensing: Features Gated by Tier

Google folded Gemini capabilities progressively into Workspace editions (after an era of separate paid add-ons, later largely absorbed into mainstream tiers — check current packaging, it moves). The durable pattern for planners: **capabilities are gated by license tier**, and rollout planning is substantially an entitlement-mapping exercise:

| Capability pattern | Typically available | Planning note |
|---|---|---|
| Gemini chat app (standalone) | Broadly, across paid tiers | The 'destination' experience |
| In-app assistance (Gmail/Docs/Sheets) | Business/Enterprise tiers | The core value for most users |
| Meet AI notes & summaries | Higher tiers | Meeting-heavy orgs feel this most |
| Advanced security/audit controls | Enterprise tiers | Gate for regulated industries |
| NotebookLM enterprise features | Varies by SKU | Research-heavy teams |

The planning discipline is identical to ChatGPT Enterprise seat planning, with one twist: because Gemini rides existing Workspace licenses, the question is less "how many seats do we buy?" and more "which tiers do our cohorts already hold, and what does each tier entitle?" An entitlement checker — license tier in, feature list out — is the tiny piece of code that answers it, and it is exactly what you will build in the challenge.

## The Full Google Stack

Gemini for Workspace is one layer of a three-layer enterprise story:

1. **Workforce layer** — Gemini in Workspace apps plus the Gemini chat app: productivity for every employee.
2. **Builder layer** — Vertex AI (previous lessons): APIs, grounding, RAG Engine, agents for your own applications.
3. **Extension layer** — Gems (custom Gemini personas), AppSheet, Apps Script hooks, and agent tooling connecting the two.

The strategic pitch is coherence: one vendor, one identity system (Cloud Identity), one admin console lineage, one data-governance story from an intern's email draft to a production RAG service. For a Google-standardized company this is the lowest-friction full-stack AI story on the market.

Now the balance. **Bundling cuts both ways**: if Workspace is your suite, Gemini is nearly free adoption-wise; if you live in Microsoft 365, this entire layer is irrelevant and Copilot occupies the slot — workforce AI choice is usually *decided by the incumbent suite*, not by model quality. **Feature-tier churn** is real: Google has repackaged AI entitlements repeatedly, and plans written against last year's SKU table age badly. And **in-app AI is harder to measure** than destination AI: no login event marks "used Gemini in Docs," so value measurement leans on admin-reported usage analytics and user surveys more than crisp funnels.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Assuming suite AI replaces builder AI. "We have Gemini in Workspace, why do we need an API budget?" confuses the workforce layer with the application layer. Drafting emails is not the same as automating claims triage; enterprises that conflate them either starve real automation projects or oversell what the suite can do.

## Rollout Notes That Differ From ChatGPT's

Most of the ChatGPT Enterprise playbook transfers (phased cohorts, champions, acceptable-use policy). The Workspace-specific additions: audit Drive/shared-drive permissions first; map cohorts to license tiers before promising features; decide Meet-recording and AI-notes etiquette explicitly (meeting summaries have social and legal texture email drafting lacks); and coordinate with the Microsoft-vs-Google suite decision above you — a Workspace AI rollout inside a company migrating to M365 is a stranded investment.

The challenge distills the licensing reality into code: a feature-entitlement checker over three mock tiers, answering the question every rollout planner fields daily — *"can this cohort actually use that feature?"* — deterministically, from data, instead of from a stale spreadsheet.

description.en: Gemini embeds into the apps, inherits their permissions, and is gated by license tier — with Vertex AI as the separate builder layer.

left.label: Suite AI covers everything — cancel the API budget

right.label: Workforce layer (Workspace) and builder layer (Vertex) solve different problems — fund both deliberately

description.en: Email drafting is not claims-triage automation; conflating the layers strands one of them.

explanation.en: Entitlements as data: each license tier maps to its feature list (mock tiers here — real SKUs churn).
The checker is one membership test — trivially auditable when someone disputes an answer.
.get(tier, []) makes unknown tiers safely feature-less instead of crashing the planner.

description.en: Entitlement questions answered from data — the daily bread of a Workspace AI rollout planner.
The Workspace-specific rollout sequence — permissions first, features second, measurement always.

instruction.en: Build the entitlement checker every Workspace AI rollout leans on. Complete has_feature(tier, feature): return True when the feature is in FEATURES[tier], and False for missing features OR unknown tiers (no exceptions). Then print EXACTLY four lines: for the three checks in CHECKS in order, '<tier>/<feature>: yes' or '<tier>/<feature>: no', followed by 'enterprise_features=' plus the number of features the enterprise tier has. Rollout planners field 'can cohort X use feature Y?' daily — answering from data beats answering from a stale spreadsheet.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
FEATURES = {  # mock tiers for training; real SKUs change over time
    "frontline": ["gemini_chat"],
    "business": ["gemini_chat", "gemini_in_docs", "gemini_in_gmail"],
    "enterprise": ["gemini_chat", "gemini_in_docs", "gemini_in_gmail",
                   "meet_notes", "security_controls"],
}

CHECKS = [
    ("frontline", "gemini_in_docs"),
    ("business", "gemini_in_gmail"),
    ("enterprise", "security_controls"),
]

def has_feature(tier, feature):
    # TODO: True if feature is in the tier's list; False for
    #       missing features or unknown tiers (never raise)
    pass

# TODO: for each (tier, feature) in CHECKS print "<tier>/<feature>: yes|no"
# TODO: print "enterprise_features=<count of enterprise tier features>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
frontline/gemini_in_docs: no
business/gemini_in_gmail: yes
enterprise/security_controls: yes
enterprise_features=5

en: return feature in FEATURES.get(tier, []) handles both missing features and unknown tiers in one line.
Convert the boolean to text with: "yes" if has_feature(t, f) else "no".
The last line is just len(FEATURES["enterprise"]) — 5 features.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert has_feature("business", "gemini_in_docs") is True
assert has_feature("frontline", "meet_notes") is False
assert has_feature("enterprise", "security_controls") is True
assert has_feature("unknown_tier", "gemini_chat") is False
assert len(FEATURES["enterprise"]) == 5

q.en: How does Google's workforce AI strategy structurally differ from destination products like ChatGPT?

en: It requires a separate Gemini login for each app
Gemini embeds inside Gmail, Docs, Sheets and Meet — the AI comes to where work already happens
It replaces Workspace apps with a single chat interface

q.en: What does permission inheritance mean for Gemini in Workspace?

en: Admins inherit every user's chat history
Gemini can access all company files to give better answers
Gemini answers only from content the asking user can already access — making a Drive permissions audit a rollout prerequisite
Permissions are copied from Vertex AI IAM roles

q.en: Why is workforce AI suite choice usually decided by the incumbent productivity suite rather than model quality?

en: Because embedded AI only delivers its near-zero-friction value inside the suite you already use — M365 shops get Copilot, Workspace shops get Gemini
Because IT departments never evaluate quality

q.en: What mistake does 'we have Gemini in Workspace, so cancel the API budget' represent?

en: Confusing the workforce productivity layer with the builder/application layer that automates business processes
